planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; perfect drainage is the primary cultural requirement — sago palms decline rapidly in heavy clay or waterlogged soils. All parts of the plant are extremely toxic to humans and pets — manganese deficiency (yellow fronds) is common in alkaline soils and is treated with chelated manganese.

directions: Plant in very well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; despite the common name, the sago palm is not a true palm but a cycad — one of the oldest surviving plant lineages on Earth, essentially unchanged since the Mesozoic Era. The dramatic, crown of stiff, glossy, dark-green fronds radiating from a thick, rough-textured trunk (which grows extremely slowly — gaining only 1–2 inches per year) makes it one of the most architecturally striking landscape plants available for zones 8–12. An established specimen with multiple trunks is an invaluable focal point in any tropical or subtropical garden. The plant produces bright orange-red seeds in large cone-like structures; every part of the plant — seeds, leaves, and trunk — is extremely toxic to humans and pets. In coastal Gulf Coast gardens it is one of the most reliable landscape plants available, tolerating salt, drought, heat, and wind with ease. Apply palm-formulated fertilizer with added manganese in spring to prevent the common yellowing from micronutrient deficiency.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Boston fern requires consistently high humidity — the fronds turn yellow and shed leaflets rapidly in dry indoor air or low-humidity outdoor conditions. In zone 9–11 it can be grown outdoors year-round in sheltered, shaded spots.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Boston fern is the world's most widely grown ornamental fern and perhaps the most popular hanging basket plant in temperate and tropical horticulture, with long, graceful, arching fronds of soft, bright green pinnae that cascade elegantly over pot edges, porch railings, and tree branches. In zone 9–12 it thrives outdoors year-round in sheltered, shaded positions on porches, under pergolas, and beneath the canopy of large trees, where the naturally high humidity and warm temperatures suit it perfectly. The key cultural requirement is consistent moisture and humidity — fronds desiccate and shed leaflets rapidly in dry, hot, or low-humidity conditions. In outdoor zone 9 culture, light frost nips the fronds but established plants typically regrow from the crown. Divide congested clumps and remove dead fronds from the crown in spring. The related 'Kimberly Queen' fern (N. obliterata) is generally more heat and drought tolerant and better suited to humid Southern gardens.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in any moist to dry, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Mexican petunia is extremely adaptable and spreading — contain with deep edging if planting near lawn or naturalistic areas, as it spreads aggressively by underground runners and self-sown seed in warm climates. Use the compact, non-invasive cultivar 'Purple Showers' or 'Katie' in contained plantings.

directions: Plant in any well-drained to moist soil in full sun to partial shade; Mexican petunia is one of the most widely used and lowest-maintenance flowering perennials in Gulf Coast landscapes, producing a continuous display of funnel-shaped flowers in purple, pink, or white from spring through frost on upright stems with narrow, dark-green leaves. Each individual flower lasts only one day, but the plant produces new flowers in such abundance that the display is uninterrupted throughout the season. It is remarkably drought, heat, and salt tolerant once established and thrives in the conditions that challenge most other perennials. However, it spreads aggressively by underground runners and abundant self-seeding — R. simplex is considered invasive in natural areas in Florida and other Gulf states. Compact, sterile cultivars such as 'Purple Showers' and 'Katie' (dwarf, 8–12 inches) are strongly recommended for residential use as they do not spread by seed. Deadhead or shear to refresh and stimulate new bloom flushes.

planting.note: Plant divisions or offsets in spring after temperatures are reliably above 65°F in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist soil in partial shade to full sun; Alocasia macrorrhiza grows vigorously in warm, humid weather and produces new leaves rapidly in good conditions. In zone 9 it may die back to the ground after hard freezes but regrows from established rhizomes in spring.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; giant elephant ear is one of the most dramatically bold-textured tropical plants available for zone 9–12 gardens, producing truly enormous, upward-pointing, arrow-shaped leaves — 2–4 feet long — on stout petioles that can reach 6–15 feet tall, creating an unmistakably tropical visual impact that transforms any landscape. Unlike the Colocasia elephant ears (which droop their leaves), Alocasia macrorrhiza holds its leaves upward and outward, creating a distinctive architectural silhouette. It thrives in the bright, filtered shade beneath large trees and palms in the hot, humid Gulf Coast climate, where high moisture, rich soil, and warmth produce the largest, most spectacular plants. The massive rhizomes are edible (raw parts are toxic due to calcium oxalate crystals — must be cooked) and are used as a starchy vegetable in Pacific Island cuisine. Remove damaged leaves promptly and fertilize monthly during the growing season for best growth.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture
FAO Plant Production and Protection Division

planting.note: Plant divisions or stem cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; blue ginger requires warm temperatures and high humidity to thrive — it does not tolerate cold, dry, or windy conditions. In zone 9 it dies back to the ground after frost but reliably regrows from rhizomes in spring.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; blue ginger is one of the most treasured and sought-after shade plants for tropical and subtropical gardens, producing dense terminal clusters of intensely deep royal-blue flowers — the rich, saturated blue is among the most vivid blues in tropical horticulture — on upright, bamboo-like stems with spirally arranged, glossy, broad leaves. The flowers appear in late summer and fall, at exactly the time when blue is most welcome in the garden palette. Despite the name, it is not a true ginger (Zingiberaceae) but belongs to the spiderwort family. It grows most luxuriantly in warm, sheltered, brightly shaded spots — beneath a pergola, porch, or canopy tree — in zones 9–12. In zone 9a it dies back completely after frost, but established rhizomes regrow reliably each spring and the plant typically blooms in its first year of regrowth. A true collector's plant and one of the best shade perennials available for the Gulf Coast garden.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep and 3–4 inches apart in spring or fall in any well-drained, moist to moderately dry soil in full sun to partial shade; white rain lily is extremely adaptable and easy to grow, naturalizing freely under lawn conditions and in garden borders. Plant in masses for maximum impact — a single bulb produces a modest display, but hundreds of bulbs blooming simultaneously after rain is spectacular.

directions: Plant bulbs in any well-drained, moist to dry soil in full sun to partial shade; white rain lily is one of the most reliable and effortless naturalizing bulbs for zones 7–11, producing masses of pristine white, crocus-like flowers — about 1.5 inches wide with yellow stamens — in late summer and fall, typically triggered to bloom within days of a summer rain after a dry period. The narrow, rush-like, grass-green leaves are present most of the year and are attractive in their own right; the plant multiplies freely by offsets to form an expanding clump over time. In zone 9 it is fully evergreen or semi-evergreen and blooms repeatedly through the warm season with each significant rainfall. It naturalizes beautifully in lawns, under trees, in borders, and in rock gardens, requiring essentially no care once established. Combine with pink Zephyranthes grandiflora, yellow Z. citrina, and salmon-peach Z. 'Grandjax' for a succession of bloom from spring through fall.

planting.note: Plant large bulbs with the neck at or just above the soil surface in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moist to moderately moist, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; grand crinum requires ample room to develop — the mature bulb can reach 12 inches across and the leaf spread 5–6 feet. Avoid disturbing established clumps as transplanting sets the plant back significantly.

directions: Plant with the bulb neck at or just above the soil surface in well-drained, moist, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; grand crinum lily is one of the most enduring and spectacular large-scale ornamental bulbs for coastal Gulf Coast landscapes, producing massive strap-like leaves and large, fragrant, spider-like clusters of white flowers with long, recurved petals and prominent red anthers on stout stems 2–3 feet tall — blooming repeatedly from spring through fall. A single well-established bulb produces a clump 4–5 feet wide with multiple bloom stalks simultaneously, creating a dramatic tropical effect. Crinum asiaticum is exceptionally salt tolerant — it is one of the few large ornamental plants that thrives in coastal beachfront conditions right to the edge of salt spray. It also tolerates periodic flooding and waterlogged soils remarkably well, making it valuable for low spots and wet areas where other plants struggle. Essentially indestructible once established; plants decades old are commonly found thriving without any maintenance in old Gulf Coast gardens.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; leatherleaf fern grows in damp, shaded sites in nature but tolerates surprisingly dry conditions once established. In zone 9 it is often semi-evergreen and benefits from protection from hard freezes.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; leatherleaf fern is the world's most commercially important ornamental fern — the glossy, dark green, triangular bipinnate fronds are the iconic 'greenery' used by florists in cut flower arrangements worldwide, with a global trade worth hundreds of millions of dollars annually. In zone 9–12 landscapes it is a superb, low-maintenance shade groundcover with exceptionally durable, leathery, handsome foliage that maintains its deep-green color year-round. It tolerates drought better than most ferns and grows in a variety of shaded conditions under trees and structures. The fronds hold on the plant for many months and cut fronds last 2–3 weeks in water, making it one of the best cut foliage plants the garden produces. Spreads slowly by creeping underground rhizomes to form a dense, weed-suppressing groundcover in shaded beds. One of the most reliable, rewarding, and practical shade plants for Gulf Coast gardens.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moist soil in full sun to partial shade; lemon myrtle grows slowly, reaching 6–8 feet over many years in most garden situations. It is frost-sensitive and performs best in zone 9b–11 where freezes are rare or light.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moist, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; lemon myrtle is an Australian native rainforest tree with the most intensely concentrated lemon fragrance of any plant on Earth — the essential oil content of the leaves (95% citral) vastly exceeds that of lemon balm, lemon verbena, or lemon thyme. Fresh or dried leaves are used in cooking exactly as lemon is used, in teas, baked goods, sauces, marinades, ice cream, and fish dishes, with a clean, pure lemon flavor that holds up better to heat than actual lemon juice. It is increasingly popular as a culinary specialty herb in Zone 9–11 gardens in the US, particularly in coastal California and the Gulf Coast. The shiny, oval, evergreen leaves are attractive year-round, and small clusters of white flowers appear in summer. It makes an excellent large container specimen in colder zones that can be moved to protection before frost. Harvest leaves fresh as needed or dry for long-term use.

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings or divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; bacopa grows naturally in wet, marshy conditions and can even grow in standing water up to 2 inches deep. It makes excellent ground cover for the margins of ponds, rain gardens, and wet areas where it spreads by creeping stems.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; brahmi (bacopa) is a small, creeping, succulent-looking wetland herb native to tropical and subtropical regions worldwide, widely used in Ayurvedic medicine for over 3,000 years as a brain tonic and cognitive enhancer — modern clinical research has substantiated its traditional use for improving memory, reducing anxiety, and supporting neurological function. The small, rounded, fleshy leaves have a mild, slightly bitter flavor and are eaten fresh in salads or cooked in the coastal cuisines of India and Southeast Asia. In the garden it forms a low mat in consistently wet or boggy conditions, producing tiny white five-petaled flowers nearly continuously in warm weather that attract small native bees. It is one of the few edible herbs that thrives in the partially shaded margins of water gardens and boggy low spots. In zone 8–9 it may die back after hard freezes but regrows from the roots; in zone 10–11 it is evergreen year-round.
sources: FAO Plant Production and Protection Division
